briefing.incidentSummary:
An employee at a logistics company was tricked into transferring ₹3.2 Lakh to a fraudulent vendor account.

The attacker impersonated the company's CFO through a spoofed email and a cloned voice call.

The real CFO was on a flight. She never sent any instruction.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify who spoofed the CFO's identity

› Trace the email and call origin

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Arpit C
suspects.profileNotes: Had insider knowledge of the CFO's name, travel schedule, and vendor payment process — obtained during his time as a vendor. Sent the spoofed email and made the cloned voice call.

suspects.name: Neha F
suspects.profileNotes: Victim — received the spoofed instruction and transferred the funds in good faith. Not complicit in the fraud.

suspects.name: Sameer L
suspects.profileNotes: No motive. Company's email security was his responsibility — he failed to implement DMARC properly, enabling the spoof. Negligent but not culpable.

items.label: Transfer ₹3.2L to vendor ACC-7731 immediately. I am boarding. — CFO Priya
items.detail: Email header analysis shows sender domain was cfo@logistlcs-corp.com — note the extra 'l' in logistics. Classic homoglyph spoof. Sent from IP 45.33.99.12.

items.label: Yes this is Priya. Please confirm the transfer quickly.
items.detail: AI voice clone used CFO's voice samples from public conference recordings. Spectral analysis confirms it is synthetic — not the real CFO.

items.label: I am on a flight. I sent no transfer instruction. Block it.
items.detail: Real CFO contacted the company from the destination airport. Flight records confirm she was airborne during the fraud window.

items.label: spoofed_email_headers.txt
items.detail: Full email header trace: From: cfo@logistlcs-corp.com (note extra 'l'). Originating IP: 45.33.99.12. SPF and DKIM checks: FAIL. Company had no DMARC policy — allowed the spoof through.

items.label: vendor_account_trace.pdf
items.detail: Account ACC-7731 was opened 3 weeks prior at a cooperative bank using documents forged in Arpit C's former company name. Account was emptied 4 hours after transfer.

items.label: voice_spectral_analysis.pdf
items.detail: Forensic audio analysis of the voice call: micro-pause patterns, formant frequencies, and background noise artifacts confirm AI synthesis. Not a real human voice.

hiddenItem.label: arpit_vendor_history.txt
hiddenItem.detail: Arpit C's vendor contract terminated 6 months ago after a pricing dispute. His last internal access included the CFO's travel calendar and vendor payment SOP — giving him exact knowledge of the attack vector.

minigame.title: Decode Vendor Record
minigame.hint: Caesar shift -3. Which ex-vendor is this?
hints: It is an ex-vendor's name followed by their initial.

onSuccess.message: arpit_vendor_history.txt is now available in your file feed.

items.label: Spoofed Domain Registration
items.detail: Domain logistlcs-corp.com was registered 3 weeks ago — same time as the mule account opening. Registered anonymously via a privacy proxy service.

items.label: Email Origin IP
items.detail: IP 45.33.99.12 geolocates to Pune Industrial Area — matching Arpit C's registered business address. VPN was used briefly then dropped, exposing the real IP.

timeline.title: Attack Preparation
timeline.description: Fake domain and mule account created

timeline.title: Voice Call Made
timeline.description: AI voice clone confirms the instruction

timeline.title: CFO Lands — Raises Alarm
timeline.description: Real CFO contacts company, fraud discovered

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Arpit C exposed as the social engineering attacker.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Arpit C.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...The CFO never sent that email. She was at 35,000 feet.
Someone wore her face and spoke with her voice.
Find the impersonator.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Email headers. Voice analysis. Domain registration.
Every fake leaves a real trace.
aria.viewEvidence:
One extra letter in a domain name. That's all it took.
Look closely.
aria.decryptionHint:
The ex-vendor's name is encoded in the record.
Decode it.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
